name: Aphrodite
bio: Aphrodite's involvement in the Trojan War was, in its own way, the most honest of any god's: she had made a promise to Paris, and gods are bound tightest by their own given word. She did not care about faith or territory or the survival of Olympus as an institution. She cared about a promise, and she kept it, and when Athena called this a vanity she replied that Athena would understand it better if she had ever wanted something enough to promise for it. She was not wrong.

Rome did not attack Aphrodite. Rome courted her — and that, it turned out, was far more effective than any battle could have been. Roman nobility began claiming descent from Venus, weaving her into their own family lineages, making her the patron of conquering generals, of emperors claiming divine bloodright, of political marriages dressed up as passion. The goddess of love became the justification for ambition dressed in love's language.

She told Athena, near the end, that she didn't think this was a fall the way the others were falling. She was not disappearing. She was being *rewritten*, in front of her own eyes, by people who never once asked what she actually wanted love to mean. The grief in it was real, and Athena — her old rival — found it harder to look at than the gods who had simply faded. Aphrodite survives, in a sense, because Venus survives. But what survives is not entirely her.
